query: clinical trial data on pentoxifylline use in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Riluzole, Edaravone, and sodium phenylbutyrate are the only approved medications to treat ALS but provide limited survival benefits. [1] [3] Riluzole, 50mg bid, is recommended for all patients with ALS and is thought to reduce glutamate-induced excitotoxicity. Riluzole is the only medication that improves overall survival (74% vs. 58% in the placebo group at 12 months) and slows the progression of symptoms (ie, survival without tracheostomy (57% vs. 50% at 18 months). Riluzole is most effective in patients who have been symptomatic for less than 5 years, have a vital capacity (VC) >60% of the predicted value, and do not require a tracheostomy. [36] It is important to note that the elimination of riluzole will be affected by CYP1A2 inhibitors like caffeine and theophylline (decrease the rate of elimination of riluzole). Transaminase elevation occurs in approximately 50% of patients. Monitor transaminases monthly for the first three months and every three months after.

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: AVP-923 (dextromethorphan-quinidine 20mg/10mg) has proven effective in patients with pseudobulbar affect. [45] Less rigorous evidence has supported the use of amitriptyline and fluvoxamine in smaller trials. Pain is treated based on its underlying cause. Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory medications, paracetamol, intra-articular steroids, or lidocaine injections are recommended for nociceptive pain, whereas gabapentin, pregabalin, tricyclic antidepressants, and morphine are advised for neuropathic pain. Mexiletine, levetiracetam, and quinine sulfate help patients with cramps. Assistive devices such as special mattresses, pillows, and wheelchairs may help to prevent pain. Ultimately, many patients require nonopioid analgesics and anti-inflammatory medications, and when these fail, opioids become the mainstay of pain treatment. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Edaravone is a free radical scavenger that is thought to reduce oxidative stress and slow functional progression. Edaravone provides the most benefit in patients with early ALS (ie, less than two years of symptoms, living independently, VC >80%, and scores of 2 or more in all items of ALSFRS-R) with probable or definite ALS (revised El Escorial criteria). One study showed approximately 33% slower functional decline in patients at 24 weeks follow-up. [37] Edaravone treatment is expensive ($146,000 annually). Edaravone should be used with caution in patients with asthma. It contains sodium bisulfite, which may cause asthmatic reactions in up to 5% of patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Pseudobulbar affect is commonly seen in PLS, and recognition with screening and early treatment with medications can reduce social withdrawal and positively impact patients' quality of life. Treatment with dextromethorphan/quinidine is commonly used. In patients who cannot tolerate dextromethorphan/quinidine or do not benefit from treatment, amitriptyline or fluvoxamine can be trialed. However, neither has been studied in controlled trials for the pseudobulbar effect. [1] Screening for mood symptoms, especially depression, is essential, and early referral to a psychiatrist or therapist is recommended when appropriate. Riluzole, a Food and Drug Administration-approved drug for ALS that provides a modest survival benefit of a few months, has not shown survival benefits in patients with PLS and is currently not recommended in the treatment of PLS. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Motor Neuron Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Future therapeutic options
retrieved_docs.content: The use of antisense oligonucleotides in SOD 1 related and C9orf related ALS are subjects of research in future clinical trials. [93]

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: There is no cure for ALS. [5] [7] The median survival is 3 to 5 years. Approximately 30% of patients survive 5 years and 10% ten years. Riluzole provides a survival benefit averaging 3 months, with edaravone showing a 33% reduction in the decline of the ALS Functional Rating Scale–Revised (ALSFRS-R) at 6 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Pentoxifylline -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify appropriate indications for pentoxifylline in the management of peripheral vascular disease. Differentiate between common adverse reactions associated with pentoxifylline and other medications used for similar conditions. Assess patient response to pentoxifylline therapy and adjust the regimen as necessary for optimal outcomes. Implement effective collaboration among interprofessional team members to improve treatment efficacy for patients who might benefit from pentoxifylline. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Pentoxifylline -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: In a double-blind placebo-controlled trial, administering pentoxifylline to patients with multi-infarct dementia showed intellectual and cognitive function improvements. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Trinucleotide Repeat Disorders -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Innate trophic support from MSC or augmented growth factor support, such as delivering brain-derived neurotrophic factor or glial-derived neurotrophic factor, is in clinical trial phases II and III for treating trinucleotide repeat disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: Trinucleotide Repeat Disorders -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Treatments That Limit the Expression of Trinucleotide Repeats
retrieved_docs.content: Several promising clinical trials are underway to identify effective methods for blocking the translation of aberrant proteins. Pharmacological approaches for treating trinucleotide repeat disorders target 3 levels of genetic expression: DNA, RNA, and protein.

retrieved_docs.title: Trinucleotide Repeat Disorders -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Stem Cell Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Marrow stromal cells (MSCs) are under investigation as a treatment for neurodegenerative trinucleotide repeat disorders that are often fatal, particularly Huntington disease. The therapy is being tested in FDA-approved phase I to III clinical trials for various disorders following promising results from extensive preclinical studies on neurodegenerative conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium phenylbutyrate is a histone deacetylase inhibitor that decreases the stress response in the endoplasmic reticulum. Combined with taurursodiol (which increases the threshold for cellular apoptosis), it decreases neuronal destruction. The result is a slower decline in function. Sodium phenylbutyrate-taurursodiol is administered intrathecally and may result in myelitis, radiculitis, aseptic meningitis, and elevated intracranial pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Evaluate the etiology of ALS. Identify the typical findings on the examination of a patient with ALS. Determine the interventions available for patients with ALS. Apply effective strategies to improve care coordination among interprofessional team members to facilitate positive outcomes for patients with ALS. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1990
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B12 may be used to treat multiple sclerosis, indicating a potential therapeutic role for this vitamin in managing the neurological disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1298
retrieved_docs.content: Dantrolen and vitamin B12 are both identified as potential therapeutic agents for multiple sclerosis, suggesting that each may be used to help manage this demyelinating neurological disorder. Their inclusion highlights the exploration of diverse treatment options in the care of patients with multiple sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2293
retrieved_docs.content: Human corticotropin and the corticosteroid formulations methylprednisolone aceponate and methylprednisolone sodium phosphate are all identified as agents that may be used to treat multiple sclerosis, highlighting the potential role of corticosteroid‑based therapies in managing this demyelinating disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1239
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone, a corticosteroid with anti‑inflammatory properties, may be used in the treatment of multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي), highlighting its role in managing inflammatory and autoimmune neurological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1364
retrieved_docs.content: Baclofen and dantrolene are both pharmacologic agents that may be used in the treatment of multiple sclerosis. These medications are considered for managing the muscle spasticity and related motor symptoms that commonly occur in patients with multiple sclerosis, offering therapeutic options to improve functional ability and quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2911
retrieved_docs.content: 4‑Aminopyridine is a pharmacologic agent that may be used in the treatment of multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي). By blocking voltage‑gated potassium channels, 4‑aminopyridine can improve nerve conduction and enhance motor function in patients with multiple sclerosis, thereby serving as a therapeutic option to alleviate some of the disease’s neurological symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1491
retrieved_docs.content: Human corticotropin, methylprednisolon acetate, and methylprednisolone aceponate are all identified as agents that may be used to treat multiple sclerosis, indicating that several corticosteroid‑related medications are considered potential therapeutic options for managing this demyelinating disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1490
retrieved_docs.content: Methylprednisolone, a corticosteroid medication, may be used to treat multiple sclerosis, offering anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects that can help reduce disease activity. In addition, vitamin B12 is also considered a potential therapeutic agent for multiple sclerosis, as supplementation may support neurological function and address deficiencies that can exacerbate symptoms. Both methylprednisolone and vitamin B12 represent possible treatment options in the management of multiple sclerosis, highlighting the importance of a multifaceted therapeutic approach.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1692
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolon, a glucocorticoid anti‑inflammatory medication, may be used in the treatment of both التصلب اللويحي (multiple sclerosis) and تَشَنُّج قَصَبِيّ (spinal stenosis). By reducing inflammation and modulating immune responses, triamcinolon can help alleviate the pathological processes underlying these neurological disorders, offering a therapeutic option for patients affected by either condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: Methylprednisolon acetát, methylprednisolone aceponate, and methylprednisolone sodium phosphate are distinct formulations of the glucocorticoid methylprednisolone that may be used to treat multiple sclerosis. Each of these corticosteroid preparations exerts anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects, providing therapeutic options for managing the inflammatory and autoimmune components of multiple sclerosis. Their inclusion as possible treatments highlights the role of methylprednisolone‑based agents in reducing disease activity and symptom severity in patients with this demyelinating disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 447
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolon is identified as a therapeutic option for treating التصلب اللويحي (multiple sclerosis), but its use is contraindicated in patients who have حالةٌ رَبْوِيَّة (a fibrotic condition). In contrast, hydrocortisone sodium succinate is indicated for the management of حالةٌ رَبْوِيَّة, providing a treatment alternative for fibrotic disorders, and it is also employed in the treatment of الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse). These relationships highlight the distinct therapeutic roles and safety considerations of triamcinolon and hydrocortisone sodium succinate in different clinical scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1823
retrieved_docs.content: Human corticotropin and several corticosteroid formulations—including methylprednisolon acetate, methylprednisolone aceponate, and methylprednisolone sodium phosphate—are identified as potential therapeutic agents for multiple sclerosis. These drugs act by modulating immune activity and reducing inflammatory processes that contribute to demyelination and neurological deficits in multiple sclerosis. Their use, particularly during acute relapses, may help alleviate symptoms, limit lesion formation, and improve short‑term neurological function, highlighting the role of corticosteroid‑based therapies in the management of this chronic autoimmune disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1530
retrieved_docs.content: Human corticotropin, methylprednisolon acetát, and methylprednisolone sodium phosphate are all identified as agents that may be used to treat multiple sclerosis. These therapies, which include the peptide hormone ACTH (corticotropin) and two corticosteroid formulations, are employed to modulate the immune response and reduce inflammatory activity that underlies the demyelinating process of multiple sclerosis. Their potential use reflects a therapeutic approach aimed at controlling disease activity and improving neurological outcomes in patients with this chronic autoimmune disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1389
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin is classified as a parasympathomimetic agent.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1622
retrieved_docs.content: N [Preparations] serves as a parent category that includes both nedokromil and nusinersen. Nedokromil is identified as a member of the class of antiallergic inhalants for obstructive airway diseases, excluding corticosteroids, indicating its use in respiratory conditions where allergy‑mediated bronchoconstriction is a concern. Nusinersen, while also belonging to the broader N [Preparations] group, is a distinct therapeutic agent used for the treatment of spinal muscular atrophy, reflecting a neuromuscular application rather than a respiratory one. This organization demonstrates that the N [Preparations] category encompasses a range of medications with different mechanisms and clinical indications, from inhaled antiallergic agents like nedokromil to antisense oligonucleotide therapies such as nusinersen.
